title: Atomic-referenced Hz-linewidth lasers via fiber interferometric stabilization
authors: Changmin Ahn, Hansol Jeong, Seoyeon Yang, Junyong Choi, Igju Jeon, Hanseb Moon, Jungwon Kim
abstract: Narrow-linewidth lasers with absolute frequency anchoring are essential for precision metrology, coherent sensing, and emerging quantum technologies beyond laboratory environments. Optical cavities and interferometers provide exceptional short-term spectral purity but lack intrinsic absolute frequency references. Atomic transitions, in contrast, provide stable frequency anchors but offer limited discrimination sensitivity. Recent hybrid approaches have demonstrated the combination of compact optical resonators with atomic references, yet achieving the Hz-level regime remains challenging. Here, we present a hybrid architecture that enables simultaneous realization of Hz-level linewidth and atomic-referenced frequency stability. An external-cavity diode laser is first stabilized to a fiber interferometer to achieve Hz-level spectral purity, while the interferometer is subsequently anchored to an 87Rb D2 transition via modulation transfer spectroscopy to suppress long-term drift and define the laser frequency relative to the atomic transition. This dual-stabilization scheme realizes a compact atomic-referenced laser with a 3.4-Hz linewidth (1-rad integrated-phase method), a minimum fractional frequency stability of 3.4x10-14 at 0.56 s, and 9x10-13 at 100 s. This architecture establishes a practical and scalable route toward compact and field-deployable atomic-referenced narrow-linewidth lasers for precision metrology and quantum technologies.
summary: The researchers developed a hybrid laser stabilization technique that combines a long-fiber interferometer with an atomic reference. This approach achieves both Hz-level linewidth and long-term frequency stability, providing a path toward compact, field-deployable lasers for quantum technologies.
main_problem: The difficulty of achieving lasers that simultaneously possess ultra-narrow linewidths (short-term spectral purity) and absolute frequency stability (long-term frequency anchoring) without the trade-off between noise and drift.
main_result: The development of a hybrid architecture that achieves a 3.4-Hz linewidth and high long-term stability, with a minimum fractional frequency stability of 3.4x10^-14 at 0.56 s.
method: A dual-stabilization scheme where an external-cavity diode laser is first stabilized to a 1-km fiber interferometer for spectral purification and subsequently anchored to an 87Rb D2 transition via modulation transfer spectroscopy to suppress drift.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of a 1560.4-nm external-cavity diode laser, a 1-km homodyne Michelson fiber interferometer, and an 87Rb D2 transition-based modulation transfer spectroscopy (MTS) system.
key_observables: Laser linewidth (1-rad integrated-phase method), frequency noise Power Spectral Density (PSD), Allan deviation (fractional frequency stability), and RMS frequency drift.
important_parameters: 3.4-Hz linewidth, 1-km fiber delay, 156/60.4-nm laser wavelength, 87Rb D2 transition, 5-Hz low-pass filter cutoff, and 3.4x10^-14 minimum fractional frequency stability.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the concept of hierarchical stabilization; Figure 2 shows the MTS setup and comparison of SAS vs MTS signals; Figure 3 details the experimental implementation of the dual-branch architecture; Figure 4 presents comprehensive performance metrics including PSD, drift, and stability comparisons.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the stability-linewidth trade-off, describes the hierarchical dual-stabilization architecture, details the experimental setup for both the interferometric and atomic branches, presents performance characterization (linewidth, noise, and drift), and concludes with a comparison to existing literature.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and precision metrology as it provides a scalable method to create the ultra-stable, narrow-linewidth light sources required for controlling atoms, driving quantum gates, and performing high-precision sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
